sources.sourceName: Introduction to Sociology, OpenStax 3e (Pressbooks adaptation) - Appendix: Chapter Quiz Answers (odd-numbered)

meta.notes: Sociology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e Ch. 16 and Ch. 17 section-quiz pages; correct answers from the OpenStax-derived odd-numbered answer key (Pressbooks CC BY 4.0 adaptation). Some Ch. 17 items reference dated world figures (e.g., Obama, Cameron) exactly as written in the source. Live OpenStax page shows CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

questions.stem: What are the major factors that affect education systems throughout the world?
questions.explanation: Resources and money are the major factors that shape education systems worldwide, affecting facilities, staffing, and access.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q1 = A.

questions.stem: Informal education _________________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: describes when students teach their peers

choices.label: B
choices.text: refers to the learning of cultural norms

choices.label: D
choices.text: relies on a planned instructional process

questions.explanation: Informal education refers to learning cultural norms and values outside a formal curriculum, rather than through planned instruction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q3 = B.

questions.stem: The 1972 case Mills v. Board of Education of the District of Columbia set a precedent for __________.
questions.explanation: Mills v. Board of Education (1972) established a precedent for access to education, helping guarantee schooling for students with disabilities.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q5 = A.

questions.stem: Because she plans on achieving success in marketing, Tammie is taking courses on managing social media. This is an example of ________.
questions.explanation: Using education to prepare for and move into a particular career path reflects social placement, a function of education that sorts people into occupational roles.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q7 = C.

questions.stem: Which theory of education focuses on the labels acquired through the educational process?
questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism focuses on micro-level processes such as the labels (e.g., 'gifted,' 'struggling') students acquire and how those labels shape outcomes.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q9 = D.

questions.stem: Functionalist theory sees education as serving the needs of _________.
questions.explanation: Functionalists view education as serving the needs of families, society, and individuals simultaneously, so all of the above applies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q11 = D.

questions.stem: What term describes the separation of students based on merit?
questions.explanation: Sorting is the educational practice of separating or ranking students, ostensibly based on merit or ability.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q13 = C.

questions.stem: Conflict theorists see IQ tests as being biased. Why?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They are scored in a way that is subject to human error.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They do not give children with learning disabilities a fair chance to demonstrate their true intelligence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They don't involve enough test items to cover multiple intelligences.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They reward affluent students with questions that assume knowledge associated with upper-class culture.

questions.explanation: Conflict theorists argue that IQ tests are culturally biased, rewarding affluent students whose background matches the upper-class knowledge assumed by the questions.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q15 = D.

questions.stem: Public schools must guarantee that ___________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: all students graduate from high school

choices.label: B
choices.text: all students receive an equal education

choices.label: D
choices.text: the amount spent on each student is equal to that spent regionally

questions.explanation: Per the source, public schools are required to guarantee that per-student spending is equitable, an issue addressed by school-funding court rulings.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q17 = C.

questions.stem: Allowing a student to move to the next grade regardless of whether or not they have met the requirements for that grade is called ____________.
questions.explanation: Social promotion is advancing a student to the next grade with their peers regardless of whether they have met the academic requirements.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 16 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q19 = C.

questions.stem: Which statement best expresses the difference between power and authority?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Authority is more subtle than power.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Authority is based on the perceived legitimacy of the individual in power.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Authority is inherited, but power is seized by military force.

questions.explanation: Authority is power that is regarded as legitimate by those who are subject to it; legitimacy is the key feature that distinguishes authority from raw power.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q1 = C.

questions.stem: In the U.S. Senate, it is customary to assign each senator a seniority ranking based on years of government service and the population of the state he or she represents. A top ranking gives the senator priority for assignments to office space, committee chair positions, and seating on the senate floor. What type of authority does this example best illustrate?
questions.explanation: Authority based on long-standing custom (such as Senate seniority) is traditional authority in Weber's typology.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q3 = B.

questions.stem: Which current world figure has the least amount of political power?
questions.explanation: As a constitutional monarch, Queen Elizabeth II held a largely ceremonial role with little real political power compared with elected heads of government or a dictator.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax (uses dated figures as written in the source). correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q5 = B.

questions.stem: Many constitutional monarchies started out as:
questions.explanation: Many constitutional monarchies evolved from absolute monarchies as the monarch's powers were gradually limited by constitutions and elected bodies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q7 = B.

questions.stem: Which of the following present and former government leaders is generally considered a dictator?
questions.explanation: Kim Jong-Un, who rules North Korea with total and unchecked control, is generally considered a dictator.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q9 = D.

questions.stem: Which is not a characteristic of a democracy?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A king or queen holds the majority of governmental control.

choices.label: C
choices.text: One goal of this type of government is to protect citizens' basic rights.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A constitution typically outlines the foundational ideas of how this government should operate.

questions.explanation: A monarch holding the majority of governmental control describes a monarchy, not a democracy, in which power rests with the people through elected officials.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q11 = B.

questions.stem: In the past, Southern states discouraged African Americans from voting by requiring them to take a _____________________ test.
questions.explanation: Literacy tests were used in the Jim Crow South as a discriminatory barrier to disenfranchise African American voters.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q13 = B.

questions.stem: Which factor does not influence voting practices?
questions.explanation: Race, social class, and ethnicity are social factors that influence voting practices; the physical voting booths themselves do not.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q15 = D.

questions.stem: Which statement best explains the meaning of "one person, one vote"?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One person should not be allowed to vote twice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A voter deserves one chance to vote.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A voter should vote only once a year.

choices.label: D
choices.text: All people's votes should count equally.

questions.explanation: The principle of 'one person, one vote' means each person's vote should carry equal weight in representation.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q17 = D.

questions.stem: Which sociologist is not associated with conflict theory?
questions.explanation: George Herbert Mead is a founder of symbolic interactionism, not conflict theory. Mills, Domhoff, and Marx are associated with conflict-oriented analyses of power.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q19 = D.

questions.stem: The Arab Spring, Occupy Wall Street protests, and the Tea Party movement have the following in common:

choices.label: A
choices.text: They sought to destroy central government.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are examples of conflict theory in action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They can only occur in a representative democracy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They used violence as the means of achieving their goals.

questions.explanation: These movements all reflect groups contesting existing power arrangements, which is conflict theory in action.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q21 = B.

questions.stem: Sociologist G. William Domhoff's Who Rules America? asserts that wealth is often necessary to exert the most influence over social and political systems. This is a ____ perspective.
questions.explanation: Domhoff's argument that wealth concentrates political influence reflects a conflict theory perspective on power.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 17 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 17 Q23 = A.
